Why do you think people hate the madden series?

There are several reasons why people dislike the Madden NFL series, despite the fact that it has a large following and generates significant sales every year. Here are some common criticisms:

1. Lack of innovation: Critics complain that the series has become stagnant, with minimal changes, improvements, or new features added in each annual release. This can create a sense of dissatisfaction among fans who expect more innovation and progress from a long-running sports franchise.

2. Annual release schedule: Some players feel that the yearly release cycle contributes to the lack of innovation, with developers focusing on publishing a new game each year rather than taking the time to make significant improvements to the existing gameplay systems.

3. Monopoly over NFL simulation games: EA has exclusive rights to the NFL license, meaning no other game developers can create official NFL games. This lack of competition can contribute to the perception that the Madden series has little incentive to improve or innovate, as there are no competing products to compare it to.

4. Focus on microtransactions: The Madden series has increasingly focused on microtransactions, particularly in the Ultimate Team mode. Some players dislike the way these microtransactions can introduce a "pay-to-win" element to the game, especially in multiplayer modes.

5. Bugs and technical issues: The Madden series has been criticized for sometimes having significant bugs and technical problems. Some players feel that the quality of the games suffers due to the annual release schedule, as developers may not have enough time to polish and fix issues before the next game is released.

6. Nostalgia for older titles: Some players have fond memories of earlier Madden games and dislike the changes made to subsequent games in the series. This can create a negative perception of the current games, even if the quality and features have objectively improved.

These reasons, along with personal preferences and opinions, can contribute to the overall negative perception of the Madden NFL series by a notable portion of players.
How should I use FText::Format in UE5?
FName to FText in ue5

To convert an FName to FText in Unreal Engine 5, you can simply use the FText::FromName function. Here's an example:

1. First, include the necessary headers in your source file:

```cpp
#include "Internationalization/Text.h"
```

2. Then, convert the FName to an FText object:

```cpp
FName ExampleName = FName(TEXT("MyExampleName"));
FText ConvertedText = FText::FromName(ExampleName);
```

In this example, we have an FName object (ExampleName) with the value "MyExampleName", and we use FText::FromName to convert it to an FText object (ConvertedText) with the same value.
How do I get the name of a GameplayAbility?

To get the name of a UGameplayAbility in Unreal Engine, you can use the UClass pointer of the ability to obtain its name, which can then be converted into an FName, FString, or FText.

Here's an example of how to do it:

1. Assuming you have the pointer to your UGameplayAbility:

```cpp
UGameplayAbility* MyAbility = /* assuming you have the pointer to your ability */;
```

2. Get the UClass of the ability:

```cpp
UClass* AbilityClass = MyAbility->GetClass();
```

3. Retrieve the name of the UClass as an FName:

```cpp
FName AbilityName = AbilityClass->GetFName();
```

Alternatively, you can get the ability's name as an FString:

```cpp
FString AbilityNameString = AbilityClass->GetName();
```

Or convert it directly to an FText:

```cpp
FText AbilityNameText = FText::FromName(AbilityClass->GetFName());
// or
FText AbilityNameText = FText::FromString(AbilityClass->GetName());
```

Please note that this method obtains the class name of the ability (such as "MyGameplayAbilityName_C") and not a display name intended for the user interface. If you need a user-friendly display name for your ability, it's suggested to add an FText property within your custom UGameplayAbility-derived class specifically for that purpose, set its value in the ability's blueprint, and then access that property when needed.

Why is my binded widget in my targeting widget null?

If the widget you bind in your targeting widget appears to be null, it might be because the widget is not initialized or accessed incorrectly. Here's a checklist to help you identify the issue:

1. Make sure the widget class reference is set in the constructor or in Defaults in the Blueprint. If you're using C++, set the class reference in the constructor of your AGameplayAbilityTargetActor_GroundTrace:

```cpp
AGameplayAbilityTargetActor_GroundTrace::AGameplayAbilityTargetActor_GroundTrace()
{
    // ...
    TargetTextWidgetClass = UMyUserWidget::StaticClass(); // Replace UMyUserWidget with the name of your widget class
}
```

If you're using Blueprints, make sure you set the class reference to your widget in the Defaults section of the AGameplayAbilityTargetActor_GroundTrace Blueprint.

2. Ensure you have the necessary widget components in your widget Blueprint or UClass. Double-check if the components inside the widget are correctly set up and named.

3. Make sure the CreateWidget function actually creates the widget instance. Check if the TargetTextWidgetClass passed to CreateWidget is valid and contains the correct UClass. Also, ensure the UMG module is included in your Build.cs file:

```cpp
PrivateDependencyModuleNames.AddRange(new string[] { "UMG" });
```

4. After creating the widget, double-check if the widget instance is added to the viewport. You should use the AddToViewport function to display the widget when the targeting begins.

5. To access the widget components from a UObject (like a GameplayAbility), make sure you use BlueprintImplementableEvent or BlueprintNativeEvent functions to communicate with the characters or other Blueprint actors that reference the widget.

6. Verify that the widget binding is done correctly. If you are using UMG, ensure that the variable in your widget Blueprint is set to be bindable (ex: TextBlock). If you are using C++, make sure you have registered a getter function using BindWidget.

7. Lastly, check if the bound widget components are being accessed at the right time during the gameplay logic. Accessing the widget before it is initialized or after it has been destroyed could result in a null reference.

Review these points to debug why your binded widget reference is null. You may need to add print statements or breakpoints to ensure your components are being initialized and accessed correctly.
